does: This paper proposes Weak-to-Strong Preference Optimization (WSPO), a method for aligning large language models by transferring alignment behaviors from weaker models to stronger ones. Rather than directly using human preference data, WSPO learns the distribution shift that occurs when a weak model undergoes alignment through preference optimization. The method captures this behavioral transformation and applies it to strengthen the alignment of larger models. Experiments on Qwen2-7B-Instruct show substantial improvements: the model's Arena-Hard win rate increases from 39.70% to 49.60%, achieves 47.04% length-controlled win rate on AlpacaEval 2, and scores 7.33 on MT-bench. The key finding is that alignment capabilities from weaker supervisors can not only transfer to stronger models but amplify in the process.
connects: This work builds on weak-to-strong generalization, a paradigm showing that strong models fine-tuned on weak model outputs can outperform their weak supervisors. It extends this concept from knowledge distillation to the alignment domain, addressing a practical challenge in preference optimization: the scarcity and cost of high-quality human preference annotations. The paper connects to preference-based alignment methods like DPO and similar approaches that learn from pairwise comparisons, while offering an alternative data source by leveraging weaker aligned models. It also relates to research on knowledge transfer between models of different capacities and on understanding what alignment behaviors learn.
enables: This approach enables more efficient alignment procedures by reducing dependence on expensive human preference labeling, potentially allowing smaller models to bootstrap alignment in larger ones. It opens questions about the nature of transferable alignment properties and how preference optimization captures generalizable behavioral patterns. Future work could explore scaling this method across multiple model sizes, investigating which alignment properties transfer most effectively, and understanding the theoretical foundations of why weaker model alignment amplifies rather than degrades in stronger models. The framework could also extend to other preference-based training paradigms or multi-step alignment pipelines where weak-to-strong transfer might compound improvements.
